claims.claim_text: The CKAD exam is based on Kubernetes v1.35, and the Application Observability and Maintenance domain includes API deprecations, probes and health checks, built-in CLI monitoring, container logs, and debugging.
claims.span_hint: frontmatter + section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: The official CKAD page names Kubernetes v1.35 and lists these observability-and-maintenance competencies.

claims.claim_text: `kubectl top` requires Metrics Server to be installed and running.
claims.rationale: The kubectl reference directly states that Metrics Server must be installed and running for `kubectl top` to work.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes Events are short-lived and should be treated as informative, best-effort supplemental data.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes glossary entry for Event explicitly describes limited retention and best-effort, supplemental semantics.

claims.claim_text: Readiness failures do not restart containers; they mark Pods not Ready and remove them from Service endpoints.
claims.rationale: The probes documentation states that failed readiness does not restart the container and sets the Pod Ready condition to false.

claims.claim_text: A startup probe prevents liveness and readiness checks from running until startup succeeds, which is the intended way to protect slow-starting applications.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Probe Strategy: Startup, Liveness, and Readiness Have Different Jobs
claims.rationale: The official probes page directly says startup probes block liveness and readiness until startup succeeds.

claims.claim_text: A Service can have no endpoints because its selector matches no Pods or because matching Pods are not Ready, and a Pod can be Running without being Ready.
claims.span_hint: section: 1. Observability Starts With Boundaries, Not Commands
claims.rationale: EndpointSlice docs explain that Services with selectors track matching Pods and that endpoint readiness maps to Pod readiness.

claims.claim_text: EndpointSlices are the backend representation Kubernetes uses for scalable Service routing, and slices for a Service can be looked up via the `kubernetes.io/service-name` label.
claims.span_hint: section: 3. Worked Example: Debug a Pod That Runs but Never Receives Traffic
claims.rationale: The EndpointSlice concept page directly documents scalable backend representation and the `kubernetes.io/service-name` ownership label.

claims.claim_text: `CrashLoopBackOff` indicates restart backoff for a repeatedly failing container; it is not a root cause by itself.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. CrashLoopBackOff: Preserve Evidence Before It Disappears
claims.rationale: The Pod lifecycle docs define CrashLoopBackOff as the backoff state for a container that keeps failing and restarting.

claims.claim_text: `kubectl logs --previous` prints logs from the previous instance of a container in a Pod.
claims.span_hint: section: 4. CrashLoopBackOff: Preserve Evidence Before It Disappears
claims.rationale: The kubectl logs reference explicitly defines `--previous` as printing logs from the previous container instance if it exists.

claims.claim_text: `kubectl top` shows recent resource consumption fetched from Metrics Server.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Logs, Metrics, and API Discovery Under Exam Pressure
claims.rationale: The kubectl top reference describes it as a view of recent resource consumption fetched from Metrics Server.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes 1.35 serves modern stable APIs such as `networking.k8s.io/v1` for Ingress, `batch/v1` for CronJob, and `networking.k8s.io/v1` for NetworkPolicy.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Logs, Metrics, and API Discovery Under Exam Pressure
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes v1.35 API reference lists these resources under those served API group/version pairs.

claims.claim_text: Modern Ingress manifests must use `networking.k8s.io/v1`; old `extensions/v1beta1` is no longer served, `pathType` is required, and backend service fields use `service.name` and `service.port`.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Logs, Metrics, and API Discovery Under Exam Pressure
claims.rationale: The v1.35 deprecation guide documents removal of old Ingress APIs and the required field-shape changes for `networking.k8s.io/v1`.

claims.claim_text: Readiness failures do not restart a container; they remove the Pod from Service endpoints until the readiness condition becomes true again.
claims.rationale: The Pod conditions docs state that Pods not Ready are removed from Service endpoints, and the probes docs define readiness failure behavior.

claims.claim_text: `k logs --previous` only works when Kubernetes still has a terminated container instance to read, so repeated restarts and cleanup can make this evidence disappear.
claims.rationale: The kubectl logs reference directly supports the previous-instance part; the disappearing-evidence wording is a fair operational inference rather than an exact quoted statement.

claims.claim_text: EndpointSlices are the scalable successor to the older Endpoints object, though `k get endpoints` is still useful for quick checks on small Services.
claims.rationale: The Service docs directly support EndpointSlice primacy and legacy Endpoints limitations; the quick-check guidance is practical advice rather than an exact documentation statement.

claims.claim_text: `kubectl top` reports metrics through the resource metrics pipeline, so it is a live usage view rather than a historical incident recorder.
claims.rationale: The kubectl top page directly describes recent metrics fetched from Metrics Server; the contrast with historical incident recording is a faithful inference rather than an explicit phrase on the page.

further_reading.title: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the canonical reference for probe semantics, restart behavior, and readiness gating.

further_reading.title: Debug Services
further_reading.why_relevant: It walks through selector, port, and EndpointSlice debugging in the same boundary-first style this module teaches.

further_reading.title: Pod Lifecycle
further_reading.why_relevant: It explains CrashLoopBackOff, restart behavior, and the Pod/container lifecycle signals used in this quiz.

notes: Excluded code blocks, diagrams, tables, quiz prompts, quiz answer blocks, hands-on exercise steps, success-criteria checklists, and purely pedagogical framing. Included the repeated Did You Know facts separately because they need distinct inline citations even when they reuse the same upstream sources.
